messages.content: The Internal Domain of the Maghrebide Rif belt comprises lower plate units, which consist of the lower Sebtides basement along with the Beni Bousera ultramafic massif, and overlying polymetamorphic granulites, gneiss, and mica-schist thrust sheets (Bouybaouen, 1995; El Maaz et al., 1989; Saddiqi et al., 1988; Gueydan et al., 2015; Michard et al., 2020; Rossetti et al., 2020, and sources cited therein). The upper Sebtides high-pressure/low-temperature metasediments lie over the aforementioned units (Zaghloul, 1994; Bouybaouen, 1995; Michard et al., 2006; Marrone et al., 2020; Marrone et al., 2021)

.

The lower Sebtides basement is mainly composed of gneisses, migmatites, and amphibolites, and it represents the oldest rocks of the Maghrebide chain, with ages ranging from 2.1 to 1.8 billion years (Ma) (Bouybaouen, 1995; El Maaz et al., 1989; Gueydan et al., 2015; Michard et al., 2020; Rossetti et al., 2020). The Beni Bousera ultramafic massif is a large body of mafic and ultramafic rocks that was emplaced into the lower Sebtides basement during the Variscan orogeny (Bouybaouen, 1995; Michard et al., 2020).

The overlying polymetamorphic granulites, gneisses, and mica-schist thrust sheets are highly deformed and metamorphosed rocks that were formed during multiple tectonic events in the Maghrebide belt, including the Variscan orogeny and the Alpine orogeny (Bouybaouen, 1995; El Maaz et al., 1989; Gueydan et al., 2015; Michard et al., 2020; Rossetti et al., 2020). These rocks have undergone several episodes of deformation and metamorphism that resulted in the development of complex structures and mineral assemblages.

The upper Sebtides high-pressure/low-temperature metasediments are characterized by high-pressure mineral assemblages indicative of subduction zones, such as eclogites, blueschists, and lawsonite- and glaucophane-bearing rocks (Zaghloul, 1994; Bouybaouen, 1995; Michard et al., 2006; Marrone et al., 2020; Marrone et al., 2021). These rocks were formed during the Alpine orogeny, when the Maghrebide belt was subjected to compressional forces that resulted in the subduction of Tethys oceanic lithosphere beneath the African continental margin.

Overall, the Internal Domain of the Maghrebide Rif belt represents a complex geological setting that has been shaped by multiple tectonic events over billions of years. Its rocks provide important insights into the evolution of the Mediterranean region and the processes that have influenced the development of its geological structures.
edit please

The Internal Domain of the Maghrebide Rif belt comprises lower plate units which include the lower Sebtides basement and the Beni Bousera ultramafic massif, along with overlying polymetamorphic granulites, gneiss, and mica-schist thrust sheets (Bouybaouen, 1995; El Maaz et al., 1989; Saddiqi et al., 1988; Gueydan et al., 2015; Michard et al., 2020; Rossetti et al., 2020, and other sources). The upper Sebtides high-pressure/low-temperature metasediments lie over these units (Zaghloul, 1994; Bouybaouen, 1995; Michard et al., 2006; Marrone et al., 2020; Marrone et al., 2021).

The lower Sebtides basement is mainly composed of gneisses, migmatites, and amphibolites, and it is considered to be the oldest rock formation in the Maghrebide chain, with ages ranging from 2.1 to 1.8 billion years (Bouybaouen, 1995; El Maaz et al., 1989; Gueydan et al., 2015; Michard et al., 2020; Rossetti et al., 2020). The Beni Bousera ultramafic massif is a large body of mafic and ultramafic rocks that was inserted into the lower Sebtides basement during the Variscan orogeny (Bouybaouen, 1995; Michard et al., 2020).

The overlying polymetamorphic granulites, gneiss, and mica-schist thrust sheets are heavily deformed and metamorphosed rocks that formed during multiple tectonic events in the Maghrebide belt, including the Variscan and Alpine orogenies (Bouybaouen, 1995; El Maaz et al., 1989; Gueydan et al., 2015; Michard et al., 2020; Rossetti et al., 2020). These rocks underwent several cycles of deformation and metamorphism which resulted in the development of intricate structures and mineral assemblages.

The upper Sebtides high-pressure/low-temperature metasediments are characterized by specific mineral assemblages indicative of subduction zones, such as eclogites, blueschists, and lawsonite and glaucophane-bearing rocks (Zaghloul, 1994; Bouybaouen, 1995; Michard et al., 2006; Marrone et al., 2020; Marrone et al., 2021). These rocks formed during the Alpine orogeny, when the Maghrebide belt experienced compressive forces that resulted in the subduction of Tethys oceanic lithosphere beneath the African continental margin.

Overall, the Internal Domain of the Maghrebide Rif belt embodies a complex geologic formation influenced by various tectonic procedures over the span of billions of years. These rocks provide insightful details and interpretations on the evolution of the Mediterranean region and the geological structures that originated from these processes.

The upper plate is characterized by Paleozoic Ghomarides thrust sheets (Chalouan and Michard, 1990, Chalouan et al., 2008) and their alpine sedimentary cover (Maate et al., 1995; Martin Algarra et al.,2000; Serrano et al., 2007; Critelli et al., 2008, Perrone et al., 2006, Zaghloul et al., 2010; El Ouaragli et al., 2016; Perri et al., 2022), in turn, by the frontal units of the internal Domain is made of theMesozoic-Eocene “Dorsale Calcaire” thrust sheets and their syn-orogenic Chattian-Lower Miocene wedge-top deposits (Zaghloul et al., 2005a; Vitali et al., 2014, Zerdeb, 2021) resulted from the inversion of the northern paleo marginplatform of the Maghrebian Tethys (e.g., Vitali et al., 2015; Zerdeb, 2021).

The upper plate of the Maghrebide Rif belt is characterized by the Paleozoic Ghomarides thrust sheets, as described by Chalouan and Michard (1990) and Chalouan et al. (2008), and their Alpine sedimentary cover (Maate et al., 1995; Martin Algarra et al., 2000; Serrano et al., 2007; Critelli et al., 2008; Perrone et al., 2006; Zaghloul et al., 2010; El Ouaragli et al., 2016; Perri et al., 2022). The frontal units of the Internal Domain are composed of the Mesozoic-Eocene "Dorsale Calcaire" thrust sheets and their syn-orogenic Chattian-Lower Miocene wedge-top deposits (Zaghloul et al., 2005a; Vitali et al., 2014; Zerdeb, 2021), which resulted from the inversion of the northern paleo-margin platform of the Maghrebian Tethys (Vitali et al., 2015; Zerdeb, 2021).
edit please

The upper plate of the Maghrebide Rif belt is mainly composed of the Paleozoic Ghomarides thrust sheets, as described by Chalouan and Michard (1990) and Chalouan et al. (2008), and their Alpine sedimentary cover (Maate et al., 1995; Martin Algarra et al., 2000; Serrano et al., 2007; Critelli et al., 2008; Perrone et al., 2006; Zaghloul et al., 2010; El Ouaragli et al., 2016; Perri et al., 2022). The frontal units of the Internal Domain comprise the Mesozoic-Eocene "Dorsale Calcaire" thrust sheets and their syn-orogenic Chattian-Lower Miocene wedge-top deposits (Zaghloul et al., 2005a; Vitali et al., 2014; Zerdeb, 2021), which resulted from the inversion of the northern paleo-margin platform of the Maghrebian Tethys (Vitali et al., 2015; Zerdeb, 2021).

The Maghrebian Flysch Basin (MFB) is widespread, located in the northern west Gondwana passive margin, and continues eastward to Sicily and southern Apennines (Guerrera et al., 2005; 2020 and reference therein). The MFB was filled by thick sedimentary series of more than 2000m thick,which start with lower Jurassic pelagic deposits and are topped by siliciclastic-turbidites of the Burdigalian age (Gubeli et al.,1984; Durand Delga et al., 1999; Zaghloul et al., 2007; De Capoa et al., 2007; Fornelli et al.,2020; Abbassi et al., 2022; Critelli 2020; Daudet et al., 2020).

The Maghrebian Flysch Basin (MFB) is a widely distributed sedimentary basin located in the northwestern Gondwana passive margin and extending eastward to Sicily and the southern Apennines (Guerrera et al., 2005; 2020 and relevant references). It is filled with thick sedimentary series that exceed 2000 meters in thickness, beginning with lower Jurassic pelagic deposits and ending with siliciclastic turbidites of Burdigalian age (Gubeli et al., 1984; Durand Delga et al., 1999; Zaghloul et al., 2007; De Capoa et al., 2007; Fornelli et al., 2020; Abbassi et al., 2022; Critelli 2020; Daudet et al., 2020).
edit grammar, spelling and vocabulary

The Maghrebian Flysch Basin (MFB) is a widespread sedimentary basin located in the northwestern Gondwana passive margin that extends eastward to Sicily and the southern Apennines (Guerrera et al., 2005; 2020; and references therein). It is filled with thick sedimentary series that exceed 2000 meters in thickness, starting with lower Jurassic pelagic deposits and ending with siliciclastic turbidites of Burdigalian age (Gubeli et al., 1984; Durand Delga et al., 1999; Zaghloul et al., 2007; De Capoa et al., 2007; Fornelli et al., 2020; Abbassi et al., 2022; Critelli 2020; Daudet et al., 2020).

This Basin was considered as a part of the Burdigalian Flysch Basin foreland system squeezed between the Internal and External zones (Abbassi et al., 2021), and which was supplied from both African and European promontories (Wildi et al.,1983; Azdimoussa et al.,2019; Daudet et al.,2020; Pratt et al.,2015; Abbassi et al.,2021). Eastward, equivalent successions filled the Lucanian-Ligurian Oceanic belt, which with the Maghrebian Flysch Basin, linked the Alpine Tethys to the Atlantic Oceanic realm since early Jurassic time.

The Maghrebian Flysch Basin (MFB) was considered part of the Burdigalian Flysch Basin foreland system, sandwiched between the Internal and External zones, as noted by Abbassi et al. (2021). Sediments in the basin were transported from both African and European promontories (Wildi et al., 1983; Azdimoussa et al., 2019; Daudet et al., 2020; Pratt et al., 2015; Abbassi et al., 2021). To the east, similar sedimentary successions filled the Lucanian-Ligurian Oceanic belt, which, along with the MFB, connected the Alpine Tethys to the Atlantic Oceanic realm since the early Jurassic period.

The Maghrebian Flysch Basin (MFB) is considered a part of the Burdigalian Flysch Basin foreland system that was squeezed between the Internal and External zones (Abbassi et al., 2021). Sediments in the basin were supplied from both African and European promontories (Wildi et al., 1983; Azdimoussa et al., 2019; Daudet et al., 2020; Pratt et al., 2015; Abbassi et al., 2021). Moving eastward, similar sedimentary successions filled the Lucanian-Ligurian Oceanic belt, which, along with the MFB, connected the Alpine Tethys to the Atlantic Oceanic realm since the early Jurassic period.

The External Rif regroups the Intrarif, Mesorif, and Prerif sub-domains originated from the former North-west African margin (e.g., Wildi, 1983; Chalouan et al., 2008), and were involved during the Miocene within the accretionary wedge to give rise to the present-day architecture of the Rif belt (Abbassi et al., 2020 and reference therein).

The External Rif is comprised of the Intrarif, Mesorif, and Prerif sub-domains, originating from the former North-west African margin (Wildi, 1983; Chalouan et al., 2008). These sub-domains were incorporated into the accretionary wedge during the Miocene, resulting in the present-day architecture of the Rif belt (Abbassi et al., 2020; and references therein).
please edit grammar, vocabulary and spelling

The External Rif consists of three sub-domains: Intrarif, Mesorif, and Prerif. These sub-domains originated from the former North-west African margin (Wildi, 1983; Chalouan et al., 2008) and were involved in the accretionary wedge during the Miocene, leading to the present-day architecture of the Rif belt (Abbassi et al., 2020; and references therein).

Mesorif sub-domain includes all Mesorif units and gathering also the Auct. “Internal Prerif” (Gimeno-Vives et al.,2020). They crop out between the Nekor left–lateral regional fault and the external Prerif, and they display a thick sedimentary series ranging from Triassic to Lower Miocene (Wildi, 1983; Frizon de Lamotte, 1985). These units are usually sealed by middleMiocene and late Miocene Wedge-Top basins (see Achelhi et al., 2016, Capella et al., 2018, El Mourabet et al., 2018b, Abbassi et al.,2020).

The Mesorif sub-domain includes all Mesorif units and also encompasses the Auct. "Internal Prerif" (Gimeno-Vives et al., 2020). They are located between the Nekor left-lateral regional fault and the External Prerif and show a thick sedimentary series spanning from the Triassic to the Lower Miocene (Wildi, 1983; Frizon de Lamotte, 1985). These units are generally overlain by middle and late Miocene Wedge-Top Basins (see Achelhi et al., 2016; Capella et al., 2018; El Mourabet et al., 2018b; Abbassi et al., 2020).

The Mesorif sub-domain comprises all Mesorif units and also includes the Auct. "Internal Prerif" (Gimeno-Vives et al., 2020). These units are located between the Nekor left-lateral regional fault and the External Prerif, and they display a thick sedimentary series ranging from the Triassic to the Lower Miocene (Wildi, 1983; Frizon de Lamotte, 1985). In general, these units are overlain by middle and late Miocene Wedge-Top Basins (references: Achelhi et al., 2016; Capella et al., 2018; El Mourabet et al., 2018b; Abbassi et al., 2020).
